stem: Find the average value of the function \( f(x) = x^{2} + ([[p]])x + ([[q]]) \) on the interval from \( x = [[a]] \) to \( x = [[b]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the integral itself, without dividing by the interval length \( [[b]]-[[a]] \).
This evaluates \( f \) at the midpoint, which equals the average only for linear functions.
This averages the endpoint values of \( f \), not the function over the interval.
This is off by one from the correct average.
This is off by one from the correct average.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[m]]x + [[k]] \). By the Mean Value Theorem for integrals, there is a value \( c \) in \( ([[a]],[[b]]) \) at which \( f(c) \) equals the average value of \( f \) on \( [[[a]],[[b]]] \). Find \( c \).
solution: Since \( f \) is linear, its average value over \( [[[a]],[[b]]] \) is \( \dfrac{f([[a]])+f([[b]])}{2} = [[avg]] \). Solving \( [[m]]c+[[k]] = [[avg]] \) gives \( c = [[answer]] \), the midpoint of the interval.
distractor_rationales: This is the left endpoint of the interval, not the point where \( f \) equals its average.
This is the right endpoint of the interval, not the point where \( f \) equals its average.
This is the average VALUE of \( f \), not the input \( c \) that produces it.
This solves \( mc = \text{avg} \) but forgets to subtract \( k \) correctly first.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( c \).

stem: Find the average value of \( f(x) = \dfrac{[[k]]}{x} \) on the interval from \( x = [[a]] \) to \( x = [[b]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates \( f \) at the midpoint, which equals the average only for linear functions.
This averages the endpoint values of \( f \), not the function over the interval.
This is the integral itself, without dividing by the interval length.
This is the plain (unaveraged) integral, missing the division by \( [[b]]-[[a]] \).
This is off by one from the correct average.

stem: Find the average value of \( f(x) = [[A]]\sin(x) + [[B]] \) on the interval \( [0,\pi] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the peak value \( \sin(\pi/2)=1 \) instead of averaging over the whole interval.
This forgets the factor of 2 that comes from \( \int_{0}^{\pi}\sin x\,dx = 2 \).
This drops the contribution of the \( \sin(x) \) term entirely.
This forgets to divide by \( \pi \), the length of the interval.
This is off by one from the correct average.

stem: Find the average value of \( f(x) = |x - [[h]]| \) on the interval from \( x = [[a]] \) to \( x = [[b]] \).
solution: The average value is \( \dfrac{1}{[[b]]-[[a]]}\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}|x-[[h]]|\,dx = [[answer]] \); because \( f \) has a corner at \( x=[[h]] \), this is NOT simply \( f \) evaluated at the midpoint.
distractor_rationales: This evaluates \( f \) at the midpoint, which equals the average only for linear (non-V-shaped) functions.
This averages the endpoint values of \( f \), not the function over the interval.
This is the plain (unaveraged) integral, missing the division by \( [[b]]-[[a]] \).
This has the wrong sign for an average value that must be non-negative.
This is off by one from the correct average.
